description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.

summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Adolph Menjou
Handwritten on photo: 1491-90
Image caption: Damon Runyon's "Little Miss Marker" with Adolphe Menjou, Dorothy Dell, Charles Bickford and Shirley Temple.

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Adolphe Menjou (often credited as Adolph Menjou) began his career in silent films. He appeared in over 130 feature length movies between 1916 and 1960, including 7 for Columbia Pictures (dates spanning 1932-1952).
Paramount produced and distributed Little Miss Marker (1934). This publicity photo was likely re-photographed between 1939 and 1952.

title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, with actor and comedian Jack Carson and two others, circa 1953-1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Connie Towers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Towers appeared as a singer in stage shows with actor/comedian Jack Carson circa 1953-1955. She also appeared on his television program, The Jack Carson Show (1954).

Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).

title_tesim: Grocery store set for Charlie Murray and George Sidney film, circa 1930-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Charlie Murray. George Sidney.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Charlie Murray and George Sidney starred in films together in the 1920's and 1930's. They appeared in one full-length film produced by Columbia Pictures: Around the Corner (1930), as well as six comic shorts produced by Columbia Pictures and released in 1934.

The set includes a counter with a scale on it, as well as shelves filled with food (Borden's evaporated milk, Karo, Willapoint oysters, S and W baked beans, Campbell's pork and beans, Cudahy's spiced corned beef, Morton's salt), with boxes of Morton's salt, Kennel King dog food, and Sunlight eggs piled in front of the shelves.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, press tag on back of photo, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

Press tag: Jessie Ralph, former Broadway star, in an unusual pose – broken-down habitue of bars. This excellent photograph was made by Carl De Voy, noted camera artist. Miss Ralph’s next screen appearance will be in the Columbia film, “Biddy,” with Richard Cromwell. Miss Ralph is under contract with Columbia.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002jdkrp (front of photo)
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.
Print housed with similar images photographed by Carl De Voy, Columbia Pictures photographer.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, childhood photographs and ephmera, [rephotograped] circa 1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Connie Towers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Prints and ephemera possibly rephotographed circa 1955.

Image comprising two visible photographs of Constance Towers (b. 1933), one including another girl (likely Lucille Towers, listed with Constance in the 1940 U.S. census). Also included is a promotional announcement. Item reads: "Ritz Cafe' at Night presents Connie Towers. Joseph Settano Trio for dancing. John Gallant at the piano." Towers appeared at the Montreal Ritz Carlton three times between 1954 and 1956. Each performance was covered in Variety. Her premiere was noted in "Night Club Reviews: Ritz Carlton, Montreal," Variety, 9 Jun. 1954: 55.

Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. description_tesim: Three newspaper clippings related to the filming of Gallant Journey (1946). The one on the left is dated Sunday, April 21, 1946, but the headline and publication are cut off. title_tesim: Elliott Clawson, screenwriter, circa 1930-1939
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Elliott Clawson.

Text from the back of a print of this image: [Press tag:] Elliott Clawson, who began writing for the screen in 1913, has to his credit such screen successes as “The Kaiser, the Beast of Berlin”, “The Phantom of the Opera”, “West of Zanzibar”, “The Road to Mandalay”, “The Leatherneck”, “Skyscraper”, “Sal of Singapore”, “Body and Soul”, “Earthbound”, “Those Who Dance” and “The Little Shepherd of Kingdom Come”. Ince, Goldwyn, Fox, M. G. M., Pathe, are a few of the studios that have been clamoring for his work in recent years. He has written five originals for Bill Boyd and three for Lon Chaney. In collaboration with Howard Higgin, noted writer and director, Clawson is now developing a sea mystery story that will give Jack Holt his greatest starring role. The story is entitled “The 13th Man”.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002jfvc9 (back of a photo of this image)
description_tesim: Elliott Clawson did not write any screenplays that were produced by Columbia Pictures, including "The 13th Man." The date of the image is based on the fact that the last of the films mentioned in the text was released in 1929.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Myles Connolly, producer and screenwriter, circa 1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Myles Connolly.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

title_tesim: Unidentified Columbia Pictures employee looking at a script with a woman, 1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: George Breakston. [Negative misfiled.]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The woman may be Frances Manson, who was a story editor at Columbia Pictures from about 1931 to about 1937.
Based on the items on the desk (newspapers, magazines, scripts, reader's reports) and the novels and plays on the bookshelves, this man may be a member of Columbia's story department.
Date of image was taken from the newspaper on the desk, which is the New York Times, February 11, 1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Houston Branch, screenwriter, circa 1936
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Houston Brand.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The negative enclosure identifies this man as Houston Brand. However it seems likely this was a typo and he is, in fact, Houston Branch, who wrote the story for the Columbia Pictures films North of Nome (1936), The Blonde From Singapore (1941), and the screenplay for Mystery Ship (1941) and Klondike Kate (1943).

title_tesim: Nana Bryant, actress, and Anna Wilson, artist, posing next to Wilson's portrait of Bryant, Los Angeles, 1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: The Devil is Driving (1937) [Incorrect negative numbers "0847-0849i" on enclosure.]

On the back of a photo of this image: [Press tag:] NANA BRYANT, COLUMBIA STUDIOS CHARACTER ACTRESS, poses beside the portrait of herself which captured third prize at the annual exhibit of the Academy of Western Painters, now on display at the Los Angeles Museum of Art. Anna Wilson, the artist, is seen to the right. The portrait represents a character Miss Bryant plays in “The Devil is Driving” with Richard Dix and Joan Perry.  [Stamp:] Irv…
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jg0fq (back of photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The title of the painting is "Nana Bryant in Costume," according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Art and Artists: Americans, Federals, Orientals Show Art," 14 Feb. 1937: C11.
The painting is signed at the top, "Anna Wilson 1936." A plaque attached to the bottom of the frame reads, "third award."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Frank Capra, director, holding his Academy Award for Best Director for It Happened One Night, Los Angeles, 1935
summary_tesim: On back of photo: [Press tag:] Frank Capra, Hollywood’s No. 1 director, receives the award from the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences for the outstanding directorial achievement of 1934. The Columbia picture “It Happened One Night” was also acclaimed the finest production of the year, and its stars, Claudette Colbert and Clark Gable, received awards for their acting. Columbia received eight out of eleven first places on this night of nights!

description_tesim: M. Coates Webster wrote four films for Columbia Pictures from 1943 to 1948. Webster also worked for Columbia in the 1930's, though his work may have gone unproduced. According to the Los Angeles Times article, “George O’Brien Will Make Six Pictures in Association with Sol Lesser, Major Zanst,” [10 May 1934: 15], Webster had been, “assigned by Columbia to write an untitled original story."

title_tesim: Lawrence Pohle (probably), writer, circa 1936-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lawrence Poble [sic, probably "Pohle"]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Lawrence Pohl was writer for movies and theater. He is credited as a contract writer for the movie Breakfast for Two (1937), a story writer for The Big Shot (1937) and Lets Make a Million (1936) (AFI catalog). He co-wrote three plays which were produced from 1934 to 1941: "Preface to Love" with Thomas Ahearn, "Alley Cat" with Alan Dinehart, and "Wise Girl" with Betty Laidlaw. He also co-wrote two screenplays with Thomas Ahearn that were not produced: "Let's be Candid," and "Take the Heir," the latter purchased by R.-K.-O.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam, author, circa 1932
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Nina Wilcox Putnam (writer).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam was an author who occasionally wrote for the movies. Though she did not write any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Woes of Rich Will Be Told" [21 May 1932: A7], Ms. Putnam was hired by Columbia to write the screenplay for the adaptation of "No More Orchids."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Frances Manson, story editor, circa 1931-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Francis [sic] Manson.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image appears online identified as the screenwriter, Frances Marion. However, when compared with other images of Frances Marion, and using the identification from this image's negative enclosure, this was concluded to be Frances Manson.

Frances Manson was a story editor who, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "The Lee Side o' L. A." [1 Feb. 1934: A4], had been working at Columbia Pictures for three years as of 1934. The Los Angeles Times article, "News of the Screen: A Day of Premiers, Chief of Them Being 'Captains Courageous' at the Astor - Other Film Notes," [11 May 1937: 31], indicates that Manson had moved on to working at Major.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Lewis Milestone, director, circa 1933
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lewis Milestone (director) (with Laurence Stallings).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is in a series of photographs of Lewis Milestone with screenwriter Laurence Stallings. Milestone and Stallings did not collaborate on any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, however, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "True View of Russia to be Given in Film: Milestone, Stallings Back from Moscow Trip with Unique View," [10 Dec. 1933: B1], the two men were planning a film called "Red Square" to be produced by Columbia. The film was never made.

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character Veneranda in the play "A Foregone Conclusion," circa 1886-1880
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
Text from image: Veneranda...
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The date has been determined based on Ms. Robson's entry in Who's Who in Music and Drama (1914).

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character of Tilly in the play "The Hoop of Gold," 1883
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The role of Tilly was Ms. Robson's theatrical debut. The show was performed at the Grand Opera House in Brooklyn, New York.
The text on the image is cut off, but based on the text from image ark no. 21198/zz002jbwkj it most likely reads, "'Tilly' in Hoop of Gold."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: Ian Keith began as a Broadway actor in the 1920's and transitioned to silent movies. His stage training ensured his transition to talkies and he became a favorite of DeMille. description_tesim: Portrait photograph of Ian Keith.

Ian Keith began as a Broadway actor in the 1920's and transitioned to silent movies. His stage training ensured his transition to talkies and he became a favorite of DeMille. In the course of his long career, he appeared in at least six Columbia Pictures movies: The Truthful Sex (1926), The Street of Illusion (1928)Light Fingers (1929), Prince of Diamonds (1930), The Deceiver (1931) and Don't Gamble with Love (1936).

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Van Johnson in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Text from related press tag: Cor3-12. Tormented. “Only the moment counts with you Sarah,” says Bendrix (Van Johnson) as he torments himself with doubts about the faithfulness of the woman he loves (Deborah Kerr), in this emotional moment from the powerful David E. Rose production of “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Handwritten on photo: COR3-12A
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-1 Suspicion. Bendrix (Van Johnson), employs a private detective agency headed by Savage (Charles Goldner), when he believes the woman he loves is unfaithful to him, in this scene from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Peter Cushing in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Handwritten on photo: COR3-8A

Text from related press tag: Cor3-8 Dramatic. Sarah Miles (Deborah Kerr), ill and bewildered, is suddenly confronted by her lover, as she is helped by her husband Henry (Peter Cushing), who is ignorant of her changed emotions towards him, in this dramatic moment from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson, and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive Producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-P2. His First British Role. A new study of Van Johnson, who makes his first appearance in a British picture as Bendrix, the embittered writer who is involved in a passionate love affair with a married woman, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” in which he co-starred with Deborah Kerr and John Mills. The complex character is said to give the famous Hollywood star one of the finest parts of his career, and one that consolidates the deep sincerity he displayed in such hits as “A Guy Named Joe,” and “Battleground.” Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene, and is distributed world-wide by Columbia Pictures. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

description_tesim: Tess Gilbert does not appear in the AFI catalog. However, she refers to herself as a screen actress in "My Beauty Hint," The Reading Eagle, 22 Feb. 1933: 9; and she is identified, in the Brooklyn Daily Eagle 12 Apr. 1936: 43, as a "script girl" for the Capra film "Ladies of Leisure" (1930), produced by Columbia Pictures. She referred to herself as a script writer for the 1930 Census, which lists her place of birth as Russia.

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

Press tag: Cor3-15. No Escape. Sarah (Deborah Kerr) suddenly realizes there can be no escape and no happiness for her as she tries to hide the letter telling her husband (Peter Cushing) she is leaving him, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Van Johnson in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Text from related press tag: Cor3-4 Jealousy. Bendrix (Van Johnson) torments himself with jealousy as he questions Sarah (Deborah Kerr), the woman he loves desperately, about her mysterious absence of several days, in this tense moment from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson, and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive Producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

The California Festival Association presented "A Midsummer Night's Dream," in a series of five nightly performances at the Hollywood Bowl, starting on September 17, 1934. The second act was also performed at the Greek Theatre in Berkeley.

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-11 Passionate Moment. Sarah (Deborah Kerr) and Bendrix (Van Johnson) cling desperately to their few moments of happiness during their brief, but passionate, relationship in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful, best-selling, Graham Greene story. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character Miss Ashford in the play "The Private Secretary," 1890
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The date has been determined based on articles in The New York Times, as well as Ms. Robson's entry in Who's Who in Music and Drama (1914).

May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The text on the image is cut off, but based on the text from image ark no. 21198/zz002jbwj1 it most likely reads, "Miss Ashford in Private Secretary."

title_tesim: Joseph Schildkraut, actor, sitting with his mother, Erna Schildkraut, circa 1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Joseph Schildkraut. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Joseph Schildkraut sitting with his mother, Erna Schildkraut. Mr. Schildkraut has his legs crossed. He wears striped pants and saddle shoes and holds his mother's hand. Mrs. Schildkraut wears a shawl and holds a purse. There are other chairs around them, and there are trees and plants in the background.

Joseph Schildkraut appeared in dozens of films from the 1920's through the 1960's and won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actor for his role in The Life of Emile Zola (1937). He appeared in one film produced by Columbia Pictures: Sisters Under the Skin (1934).

summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Inez Courtney. Nitrate.
Handwritten on negative: 4.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Inez Courtney began her acting career on Broadway before transitioning into films. She appeared in many movies in the 1930s and 1940s, including those produced by Columbia Pictures from 1934-1939.

Inez Courtney wearing a top hat and holding a jack-o-lantern. She stands on one leg. There is a fence behind her with a  cat on it. She wears a halter top, shorts, and high-heeled shoes. There are corn stalks in the background.

description_tesim: The "Helen Walter" referred to on the stamp is probably Helen Walter Root (b. about 1908), who worked as a publicity writer in the film industry in the 1930s and later. She was married to Lynn Root, a screenwriter and playwright .
